paragraphs.body: I had just finished a rigorous four-hour cheer practice and was still out of breath from the conditioning my coach had us do. My neck ached, my ankles were bruised, and I felt the pressure of the upcoming competition weighing on my soldiers. I walked into my house with a look of concern on my face when I was hit with warmth and the smell of the fireplace, putting a part of my stress at ease. I eventually made my way into the kitchen and was greeted with a big smile. My dad was joyfully washing dishes in the kitchen when he started interrogating me about my practice. I said it was ok, and I kind of shrugged it off, not wanting to talk about it. He immediately noticed my uneasiness and asked what was wrong. I told him about my state competition coming up and the doubts I had about how my team and I would do. He looked at me with a calm and reassuring voice and said, “You reap what you sow.” I looked at him with a confused look and said, “What does that have to do with my nerves?” My dad said, “You put in countless hours every week into this routine. You should trust and find comfort in your preparation.” In other words, he was saying you will get (you will reap) what you put in (what you sow). You reap what you sow" is a saying I heard often growing up, reminding me that a person’s efforts can shape their outcomes. This means that your actions and what you do have consequences, either good or bad. It comes from the New Testament of the Bible, Galatians 6:7. Another way my dad would explain this saying to me is, “Imagine a farmer that would wake up late every morning, would skip days watering his crop, and never plowed the dirt. Then, when it was time to harvest, he was confused as to why he didn’t have a successful crop that season.” This meant if you don’t put in work, you can’t expect a good result. Not only did this saying instill hard-working, diligent values in me, but it also helped me to think through my actions and realize that they have consequences. Whatever you do, whether it be a person’s work, sports, or school, you will get out what you put in. For instance, consider two students who have each been assigned a school project that’s due in two weeks. Student number one started that day and put at least 45 minutes each day into her project. The second student waited till the day before the project was due and completed it in just an hour. Unsurprisingly, the first student got an A, and the second received a C. The second student was confused about how the grades could have such a drastic difference. When student number two questioned the teacher about the results she simply said, “You reap what you sow.”

annotations.text: My neck ached, my ankles were bruised, and I felt the pressure of the upcoming competition weighing on my soldiers.

annotations.comment: I'm interested in the way your father's voice speaks so clearly in this essay. Neat!
annotations.context_right: would wake up late every morning, would skip days watering his crop, and never

paragraphs.body: Now that I'm older, I realize that this phrase isn’t just referring to the work you do or put into something but can also refer to what a person says. You might be wondering how this saying relates to a person’s words or conversations? I like to think of it like this. Things we say can affect the reactions and feelings of the people we communicate with. I now think about the proverb in this type of context a lot more. I’ve often said things without thinking about the consequences, which would lead to reactions I didn’t expect. This taught me to think about my words and other people’s feelings. Understanding this has made me more intentional in everything I do. We can't expect positive outcomes if we don’t put in the effort or choose our words wisely. When I reflect on a time when I had an unexpected reaction to something, I remember I had told a substitute teacher to have a nice day and I had a short conversation with her before I walked out of class, not thinking anything of it. I saw her the next day and she said that it made her day that someone acknowledged her and that most students just walk out or in the class with their heads down not saying much. When she told me that I realized how our words can have an impact even with such a little thing as saying have a nice day.

annotations.text: Understanding this has made me more intentional in everything I do.

annotations.comment: This isn't wrong necessarily...but maybe it reads more fluidly without this line?

paragraphs.body: As a first-year student, I have noticed myself being very particular about what I say and how I present myself to people I am just meeting for the first time. I’ve been unknowingly reinventing myself. Each word that comes out is like a seed I carefully plant with an overwhelming awareness of the possible outcome. The outcome is how I'm perceived and having hopes of making new friends. For many people, coming to college is a once-in-a-lifetime experience, and with this experience, you get the chance to choose who you want to be and reinvent the person you want to be. Take advantage of being able to reap the person you want to sow.

annotations.text: The outcome is how I'm perceived and having hopes of making new friends.
annotations.comment: This sentence doesn't quite make sense to me?

paragraphs.body: Walking into my house after a long, tiring practice, I felt the weight of my nerves and doubts about my upcoming cheerleading competition. It was my dad’s words of wisdom that gave me peace. By teaching me this proverb “You reap what you sow” he reminded me that my preparation and hard work would indefinity yield a promising result. You can’t expect a good outcome with something you don’t put effort into. I’ve come to understand that there are two meanings of the saying, both with actions and words. Reflecting on this lesson has not only changed my approach to my actions but also my words as I now take time to think before I speak. As I look ahead, I realize that this will always guide me in every aspect of my life from my academic career to my relationships. I encourage you to think and reflect about your choices. Are you putting 100% into everything you do? Do you ever think about the impact that your words have on other people? The consequences of our actions, both physical and verbal, are like seeds we plant. They can take root and grow.

annotations.text: Walking into my house after a long, tiring practice, I felt the weight of my nerves and doubts about my upcoming cheerleading competition.
